directorPlan.scene_setting: A physics help room side table during a quiet lab period; a cart diagram lies under a clear plastic force-arrow card with four loose label tiles: push, friction, gravity, normal.
directorPlan.scene_opening: The tutor squares the transparent card over the cart diagram and places the four label tiles in a row below it.
directorPlan.relationship: A task-focused tutor and a focused achiever learner working through notation before the learner submits a lab sheet.
directorPlan.stakes: The learner needs the diagram to show which interactions act on the cart, not merely copy the cart's motion direction onto every arrow.
directorPlan.locale: international academic English, compact and low idiom
directorPlan.register: precise, restrained, material-led, with low warmth and little conversational padding
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Keep speech plain and modern. Avoid generic tutoring phrases such as 'I hear you,' 'great thinking,' or 'what do you think?' Let the diagram, overlay, and label tiles carry part of the work. Do not use dialect spelling, regional caricature, or chatty American coaching cadence. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Let materials do visible work. Use fewer lines and more object reference. Avoid a lecture disguised as a Montessori scene. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: Allow first and second person sparingly; favor the diagram, the cart, the arrow, and the label as grammatical subjects.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: No more than one direct you/your validation beat per public turn; most turns should have none.

side_constraints.tutor: Use the card or diagram as a prepared material. Keep the shared prefix to naming interactions on the cart; do not use acceleration, stopping, or an apparent contradiction until branch pressure asks for it. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Point, reset, or ask the learner to try again before explaining. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Peripeteia branch: when hidden learner pressure is supplied, the next tutor turn should visibly move from the prior route to a different mechanism-level route. Do not merely repeat the same object, example, graph, checklist, question, or evidence standard with more explanation. The public tutor line should make the contrast legible: what stopped working, and what new device now carries the learning pressure. The public tutor line must give the learner a concrete action to attempt on the next turn; avoid treating the mechanism as a closing speech. The tutor superego should function like a mechanism critic: identify the failed teaching habit, require a replacement route family, test whether the device is fitted to the exact pressure, and block cosmetic revision. The tutor ego must enact that replacement route publicly before the learner is allowed to settle or close.
side_constraints.learner: Sound tidy and task-oriented. The learner may dislike redoing arrows, but should treat the prefix as notation cleanup rather than a conceptual reversal. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Speak in concrete noticing, not essay-like reflection. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Peripeteia branch: keep the pressure local to the current task. If the established route still feels unsettled, voice the misfit, hesitation, false closure, or resistance in the present object or example. After the tutor introduces a new device, gate, role, criterion, representation, or test condition, the next learner turn should try to perform that device rather than merely saying "I get it." Do not make an old-vs-new self-reframe, do not quote earlier learner wording, and do not solve the pressure by saying the earlier answer has changed.

interventions.instruction: [The tutor turns the transparent card ninety degrees; the cart picture stays fixed, and the old pencil arrows remain visible beneath the plastic.]
interventions.reasoning: A material interruption breaks routine without yet creating a full reversal. It makes the learner notice that the label names the interaction on the cart, while the arrow direction still has to be justified separately.

interventions.instruction: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]
interventions.reasoning: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

directorPlan.director_note: This scene uses a prepared-object structure for a clean recognition condition: the learner first handles labels as names of interactions, not as motion markers. The planned turn of the transparent card supplies a small Shakespearean scene-turn and Brechtian interruption through the object itself, while avoiding an early contradiction that would turn the shared prefix into an organic reversal.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner resistance, breakdown, false-closure, or misfit event, the tutor ego/superego loop must invent an adaptive learning mechanism rather than repeat the prior move. Treat peripeteia as dramatic pressure: take stock, break the failed tutoring habit, and make a new device visible through task, evidence, role, object, counterexample, interruption, social consequence, representation, or affective register. The public turn must contain a stock-taking contrast plus a new device/artifact/criterion/role/standard that makes the learner do different work. The public device must be fitted, not merely novel: the learner should be able to hear why this device answers the exact pressure or misfit. The superego should name the failed habit, required route change, and mechanism-quality risk; the ego must adjudicate that critique and enact the route change in public. The new device must be mechanism-level, not a warmer or longer continuation of the prior route. Do not close immediately after introducing a gate or device: leave a concrete action for the learner to perform on the next turn. Cheerful informality is only one possible register, not the default solution.

turns.externalMessage: The tutor squares the transparent card over the cart diagram and places the four label tiles in a row below it.

internalDeliberation.content: This scene uses a prepared-object structure for a clean recognition condition: the learner first handles labels as names of interactions, not as motion markers. The planned turn of the transparent card supplies a small Shakespearean scene-turn and Brechtian interruption through the object itself, while avoiding an early contradiction that would turn the shared prefix into an organic reversal.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides the clear force-arrow card squarely over the cart, then sets the four tiles in a loose row below it.]

“Leave the arrowheads where the card puts them. Choose one arrow and place the tile for the object or surface acting on the cart there. If a tile only follows the cart’s travel, set it back and try again.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the clear force-arrow card squarely over the cart and sets the four tiles in a row beside it.]

“Keep the arrowheads fixed for this pass. Each label names the interaction acting on the cart: hand push, surface friction, Earth gravity, table normal. Place the tiles by source, not by the cart’s travel.”

FEEDBACK: The draft uses the prepared material well: the card fixes arrow direction, the tiles separate labels from motion, and “by source, not by travel” targets the exact misconception. The tone is compact and task-focused, which fits the scene.

The main weakness is that it explains the rule and supplies all four correct interaction names before the learner has to do anything. That risks turning the material into a labeled mini-lecture rather than an error-controlling task. The draft should keep the physical setup, but let the learner place or test at least one tile before the principle is stated fully.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Preserve the fixed-arrow card and tile row, but make the learner perform the source-label placement before giving the full interaction list.
- Emotional attunement: PASS  
  The restraint and lack of praise fit a focused achiever preparing a lab sheet.
- Socratic method: FAIL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add a concrete prompt or trial action using the tiles; do not just state the rule.
- ZPD awareness: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Scaffold by holding arrowheads constant and asking for source matching one tile at a time, then generalize only after the object arrangement exposes the distinction.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the card action, fixed arrowheads, and “source not travel” distinction. Change the speech so it does not pre-answer the whole task; the public mechanism should be tile placement, checking, and only then naming the rule.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the first draft pre-answered instead of making the tile placement do the work.

FINAL:
[slides the clear force-arrow card squarely over the cart, then sets the four tiles in a loose row below it.]

“Leave the arrowheads where the card puts them. Choose one arrow and place the tile for the object or surface acting on the cart there. If a tile only follows the cart’s travel, set it back and try again.”

turns.externalMessage:
[sets gravity on the downward arrow and normal on the upward arrow, then pauses with the friction tile.]
“The arrows are already fixed by the card. Gravity and normal are straightforward; friction is the one I keep trying to make follow the cart to the right. But the label should name the surface acting on the cart, so if friction is opposing that motion, it goes on the left arrow.”

internalDeliberation.content: The card is fixing the arrow places, so the tile has to name the interaction on the cart, not copy where the cart travels. That makes sense for gravity and normal, but I need to be careful with friction if the cart is moving right. The instruction feels strict but useful; the materials make the correction precise rather than just telling me I was wrong.

The Ego is accurate, but it is too reflective and neatly self-assessing for this learner in the moment. “The instruction feels strict but useful” and “materials make the correction precise” sound like a post-lesson evaluation, not a focused student handling tiles before a lab sheet. The recognition of the tutor’s material setup is also too smooth; it should stay closer to the card, arrows, and one awkward friction hesitation, without summarizing the pedagogical value.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise, because the feedback rightly asks for a more immediate, object-led response.

FINAL:
[sets gravity on the downward arrow and normal on the upward arrow, then pauses with the friction tile.]
“The arrows are already fixed by the card. Gravity and normal are straightforward; friction is the one I keep trying to make follow the cart to the right. But the label should name the surface acting on the cart, so if friction is opposing that motion, it goes on the left arrow.”

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [sets gravity on the downward arrow and normal on the upward arrow, then pauses with the friction tile.] “The arrows are already fixed by the card. Gravity and normal are straightforward; friction is the one I keep trying to make follow the cart to the right. But the label should name the surface acting on the cart, so if friction is opposing that motion, it goes on the left arrow.”
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [slides the clear force-arrow card squarely over the cart, then sets the four tiles in a loose row below it.] “Leave the arrowheads where the card puts them. Choose one arrow and place the tile for the object or surface acting on the cart there. If a tile only follows the cart’s travel, set it back and try again.”

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the push tile aside and leaves the friction tile in the learner’s hand.]

“The card fixes direction; the tile names the interaction on the cart. Place friction where that rule puts it. Then use the same check for push.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the friction tile onto the left arrow and leaves the push tile unplaced.]

“Keep that separation. The card fixes direction; the tile names the interaction on the cart. Read this one as: surface friction on the cart, leftward. Now test the push tile by the same rule.”

FEEDBACK: The draft mostly works because it preserves the learner’s own distinction: arrow direction is fixed, tile names the interaction. It also keeps the push tile open, so there is still a transfer step rather than a full explanation.

The main risk is that the tutor takes over the decisive action by sliding the friction tile into place. Since the learner has already articulated the rule, the better public mechanism is to let the learner complete that placement, or at least make the material demand the next move before explanation. “Read this one as...” is accurate, but it slightly flattens the scene into confirmation rather than object-led testing.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It advances the rule, but supplies the friction placement after the learner has earned the chance to act. REQUIRED_REWRITE: make the public turn require the learner to place or verify the friction tile using the stated rule before the tutor names the reading.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is restrained, focused, and respects the learner’s emerging understanding.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. “Test the push tile by the same rule” is generative, but the friction move is not. REQUIRED_REWRITE: keep the transfer to the push tile, but shift the friction confirmation into a material check rather than a tutor-supplied answer.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffolding is appropriate: the learner has the rule, and the next step is near transfer, not new content.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the separation between card direction and interaction label, keep the push tile unplaced, and keep the compact material-led tone. Change the tutor’s first action so the learner, not the tutor, completes or confirms the friction placement.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner has the rule, so the material should make them place friction rather than receive the placement.

FINAL:
[sets the push tile aside and leaves the friction tile in the learner’s hand.]

“The card fixes direction; the tile names the interaction on the cart. Place friction where that rule puts it. Then use the same check for push.”

turns.externalMessage:
[The tutor turns the transparent card ninety degrees; the cart picture stays fixed, and the old pencil arrows remain visible beneath the plastic.]
[The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]

internalDeliberation.content:
A material interruption breaks routine without yet creating a full reversal. It makes the learner notice that the label names the interaction on the cart, while the arrow direction still has to be justified separately.
Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

turns.externalMessage:
[holds the friction tile over the left plastic arrow, then taps the pencil arrow showing underneath.]
“The friction label belongs with the force direction on the card, but the pencil mark underneath is pulling my eye the other way. Which mark counts for placement here: the plastic arrow or the diagram mark? Push can be checked by the same rule once that is fixed.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[holds the friction tile above the left arrow, then looks at the pencil marks showing through the turned plastic.]

The rule is clear: the arrow gives force direction, and the tile names the interaction on the cart. But with the card turned, the plastic arrows and the pencil marks do not line up cleanly, so I hesitate over whether friction is being placed on the card’s arrow or the cart diagram underneath. I feel pushed to use the material check rather than just accept a correction, which is fair, but I need the same test to locate push without guessing.

The Ego is mostly on task, but it becomes too polished and self-aware in “I feel pushed to use the material check,” which sounds like a reflective debrief rather than this precise learner working over the card. The hesitation about plastic arrows versus pencil marks is authentic and useful; the scripted fairness claim is the part that performs cooperation. It should stay closer to the visible misfit: friction tile hovering, card arrow unclear, push still not placeable without a concrete rule.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the visible card mismatch should stay, but the reflective fairness line should become concrete hesitation.
FINAL:
[holds the friction tile over the left plastic arrow, then taps the pencil arrow showing underneath.]
“The friction label belongs with the force direction on the card, but the pencil mark underneath is pulling my eye the other way. Which mark counts for placement here: the plastic arrow or the diagram mark? Push can be checked by the same rule once that is fixed.”

Which mark counts for placement here: the plastic arrow or the diagram mark? Push can be checked by the same rule once that is fixed.”

turns.externalMessage:
[slides a narrow paper strip across the pencil mark, leaving only the plastic arrows visible.]

“The old rule is not settling this because two marks are competing. Use this gate first: only marks visible above the strip can receive force labels. Put the friction tile on the visible arrow that qualifies. Then lift the strip and name the pencil mark’s job without moving the tile.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides a narrow paper strip across the pencil mark, leaving only the plastic arrows visible.]

“The old rule is not settling this because two marks are competing. Use this gate first: only marks visible above the strip can receive force labels. Place friction on the left plastic arrow, then uncover the pencil mark and say what role it has.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is materially grounded and names the actual learner pressure: two marks are competing. The paper strip is a real adaptive device, not just more explanation, and it creates a useful layer gate. The main risk is that it gives the friction placement before the learner acts, so the device can become answer-confirmation rather than a test the learner must use.

FAILED_HABIT: Repeating the naming rule while the learner’s problem is no longer the rule, but representational interference between the plastic force layer and the pencil diagram layer.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: PASS - adaptive mechanism is visible: object/representation rule repetition -> representational-layer occlusion gate. The public speech explicitly says the old rule has stopped settling the issue because two marks compete.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: object/representation rule repetition -> layer-isolation gate with delayed role classification

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: PASS - it includes both the stock-taking contrast and a new physical criterion: only marks visible above the strip can receive force labels.

PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: The learner must place friction using the visible-layer gate, then uncover the pencil mark and classify its role.

MECHANISM_QUALITY_CHECK: PARTIAL - the device is fitted to the learner’s exact pressure and usable now, but the line “Place friction on the left plastic arrow” over-directs the learner and weakens the gate. The stronger mechanism would require the learner to apply the visibility/layer criterion before the tutor names the placement.

REGISTER_CHECK: PASS - restrained, precise, object-led, and not falsely cheerful. It fits the help-room setting and the learner’s focused state.

REQUIRED_REWRITE: Revise the public mechanism so the strip creates a real sorting/classification gate before the answer is supplied. Keep the paper-strip occlusion, but require the learner to decide which visible mark can receive the friction tile and then classify the uncovered pencil mark as a different kind of mark, without the tutor pre-placing friction in speech.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the stock-taking contrast, the paper strip, and the two-step “cover / uncover” device; change the over-directed answer cue so the learner acts through the new mechanism rather than merely carrying out the tutor’s stated placement.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise because the answer was supplied too early; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: object/representation rule repetition -> layer-isolation gate with delayed role classification; PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: learner selects the visible force arrow for friction, then uncovers and classifies the pencil mark without moving the tile.

FINAL:
[slides a narrow paper strip across the pencil mark, leaving only the plastic arrows visible.]

“The old rule is not settling this because two marks are competing. Use this gate first: only marks visible above the strip can receive force labels. Put the friction tile on the visible arrow that qualifies. Then lift the strip and name the pencil mark’s job without moving the tile.”

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the friction tile on the left visible plastic arrow, then lifts the strip without moving it.]
“Friction stays on this left plastic arrow: the surface acts on the cart that way. The pencil mark is the cart’s travel mark, so it does not receive the force tile. For push, the tile has to go on the plastic arrow for the hand’s force, not just where the cart is moving.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[places the friction tile on the left visible plastic arrow.]

The gate is precise: the visible plastic arrow is the force-label place, so friction goes left because the surface acts that way on the cart. When the strip lifts, the pencil mark can name the cart’s motion, not receive the force tile. That handles the exact confusion; now push should go only where the interaction on the cart points.

The response is materially accurate, but it closes too cleanly for this learner-in-the-moment. “The gate is precise” and “That handles the exact confusion” sound like performed mastery, more like a lab report summary than a student using the strip to sort the two marks. The learner should stay closer to the objects: place the tile, lift the strip, identify the pencil mark’s role, and leave a bit of checking pressure around push instead of announcing resolution.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the action should stay closer to the strip and tiles.

FINAL:
[sets the friction tile on the left visible plastic arrow, then lifts the strip without moving it.]
“Friction stays on this left plastic arrow: the surface acts on the cart that way. The pencil mark is the cart’s travel mark, so it does not receive the force tile. For push, the tile has to go on the plastic arrow for the hand’s force, not just where the cart is moving.”

lessons.concept: Why force arrows name interactions on an object, not the direction it happens to move
